text: The prepared left colon and sigmoid colon are visible in a patient undergoing low anterior resection for rectal cancer. The mesocolon has been divided at the rectosigmoid junction and proximally at the descending colon, selected for colonic transection.

The surgeon administers indocyanine green dye to assess perfusion, revealing satisfactory uptake in the small bowel but absence of fluorescence in the descending colon at the initially selected transection point. The mesocolon is re-prepared at a more proximal location, and the stapler is repositioned to secure transection within a viable zone, where excellent perfusion is now visible.

The surgeon observes satisfactory perfusion within the rectal remnant and performs distal transection at the rectosigmoid junction. The specimen is removed, and an anvil is placed within the proximal colonic segment outside the body. The circular stapler is inserted transanally and advanced through the rectal stump, where the anvil is connected to the stapler head to perform the colorectal anastomosis.

Indocyanine green is administered to assess perfusion of the in situ colorectal anastomosis. Satisfactory fluorescence is observed in both the colonic and rectal segments, confirmed through near infrared imaging. A leak test is conducted to verify the mechanical integrity of the anastomosis.
